topic: GenAI for feature proposals; the meaningless-feature risk
scope: LLM-proposed features (academic primary: CAAFE); plausible-but-spurious features; why automation needs a validator.
key_concepts: The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) to inject domain knowledge into automated feature engineering by reading column names and dataset descriptions.
Introduction to Context-Aware Automated Feature Engineering (CAAFE) as a method where generative AI writes feature transformation code based on semantic context.
The inherent risk of LLMs in this domain: proposing plausible-but-spurious features. LLMs generate features that sound logical but may hold no statistical signal in the actual data.
The absolute necessity of an automated validation step (e.g., cross-validation against a baseline) to filter out meaningless features proposed by GenAI.

definitions.meaning: Context-Aware Automated Feature Engineering; an approach that uses language models to semantically interpret a dataset and generate code for new domain-specific features.
A generated feature that makes perfect semantic sense in human language but provides no actual predictive value or introduces noise into the model.

examples: An LLM reading a real estate dataset might propose 'Distance to City Center divided by Property Age'. This sounds analytically clever, but if the dataset's target is predicting short-term rental yields, the feature might be entirely uncorrelated and merely bloat the feature space.
exclusions: Do not discuss transformer architectures, tokenization, or how LLMs are trained. Focus exclusively on their role as feature proposers.
Do not present GenAI as a standalone solution without the validation step.
